t: Marked fear in one or more social situations — being observed or evaluated by others.
The patient fears humiliation, rejection by others, or harming others.
Avoidance or endurance with intense discomfort.
Fear is disproportionate to the social situation.
Not fully explained by another mental disorder or medical condition.
DSM-5-TR — "performance only" specifier (limited to performance situations).
ICD-11 — parallel; in children, peer setting is also assessed (not only with adults).
NICE CG159 (Social anxiety disorder: recognition, assessment and treatment, 2013) — individual CBT first-line.
Liebowitz Social Anxiety Scale (LSAS), SPIN (Social Phobia Inventory) — scale and monitoring.
Difficult escape, help absent; social evaluation absent.
Persistent pattern, broader; comorbidity is common.
Social-communication deficit early onset; social interest variable.
Loss of speech in a specific context.
— redirecting self-focused attention, video feedback, behavioral experiments, cognitive restructuring, in vivo exposure. 12–16 sessions. NICE CG159 — individual CBT superior to group CBT.
– escitalopram, sertraline, paroxetine (FDA approved), venlafaxine (FDA approved).
moclobemide (RIMA, approved in Europe), pregabalin (evidence limited), gabapentin.
— only short-term for a specific situation (e.g., prior to performance); not sustained.
— in performance subtype, 1–2 hours before performance; ineffective in social reciprocal interaction.
— as a form of self-medication; requires special attention and parallel intervention.
NICE CG159 (2013) — individual CBT (Clark) first-line; SSRIs alternative; group CBT or self-help may be suitable for some patients.
CANMAT 2014 — first-line escitalopram, sertraline, paroxetine, venlafaxine.
Mayo-Wilson E. et al. Lancet Psychiatry 2014 network meta-analysis — individual CBT has the greatest effect; SSRIs second.

— Specifically developed for social anxiety; external redirection of self-focused attention, video feedback (seeing one's performance through others' eyes), behavioral experiments (testing fearful beliefs), in vivo exposure. Evidence: Clark D.M. et al. J Consult Clin Psychol 2003 RCT — experimental individual CBT superior to other interventions.
— 12-session group format; Heimberg R.G. model. Effective, but relatively weaker than individual CBT (NICE CG159).
Andersson G. et al. Comparison of self-help internet-based cognitive behavioral therapy (ICBT) with therapist-supported ICBT to individual cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT).
Liebowitz Social Anxiety Scale (LSAS — Liebowitz Social Anxiety Scale)
Chronic course, high relapse rate without treatment.
CBT and SSRIs result in significant improvement in most patients.
Early onset and avoidant personality comorbidity as a poor prognosis marker.
Myth 1: "Social anxiety — ordinary 'shyness about life', does not require treatment"
Social anxiety disorder is associated with functional impairment, academic and occupational limitations, risk of depression and alcohol use; it is evaluated as a medical condition.
Myth 2: "The patient can live a normal life by completely avoiding social situations"
Avoidance symptoms strengthen and lead to isolation; exposure is the main therapeutic mechanism.
Alcohol provides short-term relief but long-term exacerbates distress and leads to alcohol use disorder; prevalence of this combination is 20–40%.
Myth 4: "Benzodiazepines are effective long-term for social anxiety"
Short-term effect; addiction potential; CBT or SSRIs superior.
Myth 5: "Propranolol is effective for all forms of social anxiety"
(effective before exit); ineffective in social interaction (party, relationships).
Myth 6: "The patient has avoidant personality disorder, so treatment is unresponsive"
Comorbid avoidant personality patient may also respond to standard interventions; long-term CBT or schema therapy.
Myth 7: Only hypnotherapy or NLP cures quickly
No evidence base; CBT and SSRIs are gold standard.
Myth 8: Cannabis (CBD) is a safe alternative for social anxiety
initial studies exist, but insufficient for clinical recommendation; THC may worsen distress.
